1: tselem (ṣelem, Aramaic): image, statue. The same word used in Daniel 2:31 for the statue in Nebuchadnezzar's dream — whose head was gold. Here Nebuchadnezzar makes an entire image of gold, apparently extending the gold-head glory to the whole statue. The dimension ratio (60 cubits tall, 6 cubits wide — roughly 90 × 9 feet) is unusually narrow; some commentators suggest a statue on a pedestal. The plain of Dura (biqdath dura, the plain of Dura) is unidentified but the name suggests a walled enclosure. The Babylonian setting is historically plausible: large royal cultic images are attested in Neo-Babylonian texts.
2: rab-ravravan (rab-rabrĕwān): an honorific superlative — the most exalted leaders. The list in Daniel 3:2-3 includes seven categories of Babylonian court officials (satraps, prefects, governors, counselors, treasurers, justices, magistrates). The sevenfold list is comprehensive — the whole administrative hierarchy is summoned. The political intent is total: Nebuchadnezzar wants universal imperial compliance with the dedication.
3: The repetition of the full official list from Daniel 3:2 in Daniel 3:3 is a deliberate literary device — the narrator emphasizes both the command and its compliance with the same catalogue. The phrase qamah letsel tsalma (qāmāh leṣēl ṣalmāʾ: stood before the image) uses the spatial verb qum (stand/arise) — they have physically placed themselves in the posture of deference before the image. The repetition creates the impression of mass, relentless pressure.
4: qara (qārēʾ): the herald cried out. The Aramaic karoz (herald, Daniel 3:4) is a loanword from Persian kāruza or Greek kēryx — one of Daniel's Aramaic Persian/Greek loanwords that scholars note. The announcement formula (lekhon amreen ammin umevaya uleshanaya: “to you it is commanded, peoples and nations and languages”) uses the triadic nations-formula that appears throughout the book (Daniel 2:44; Daniel 3:4; Daniel 4:1; Daniel 5:19; Daniel 6:25; Daniel 7:14) — every empire-spanning utterance in Daniel reaches all peoples, nations, and languages, the same universal formula the NT applies to Christ's dominion (Revelation 5:9; Daniel 7:9).
5: nefila' (nip̄lûn, imperfect): you will fall and worship. The imperfect in Aramaic carries imperatival force here (a future-as-command). sagad (sĕgid): to prostrate oneself in worship — the technical term for cult-prostration throughout Daniel 3, occurring eleven times in the chapter. The instruments listed (qarna, mashroqita, qathros, sabbeka, psanterin, sumponeyya) include a mix of Aramaic/Semitic and Greek loanwords (qathros = Greek kithara, harp; psanterin = Greek psalterion; sumponeyya = Greek symphōnia, bagpipes or harmony) — evidence of the Persian-Greek cultural milieu, exactly what would be expected in a sixth-fifth century Babylonian court.
6: atun nura (attûn nûrāʾ): furnace of fire. The atun is a large smelting/burning furnace, likely a brick kiln similar to those used for the massive Babylonian building projects. The phrase yaqidta (burning hot) emphasizes intensity. The death penalty as immediate and public — without trial — is the political function of a cultic test: refusal is treated as sedition, not just impiety. The furnace as execution device is attested in ancient Near Eastern legal contexts (Jeremiah 29:22 refers to Zedekiah's officials being roasted by Nebuchadnezzar).
7: The response of the entire assembly: sagad — they prostrated themselves and worshipped the image. The Aramaic construction nefila sagdeen (falling, worshipping) uses two simultaneous participles describing a single act: the physical prostration is the worship. The mass compliance of “all the peoples, nations, and languages” at the sound of music underscores the social pressure facing the three men — the entire world system bows. The counter-narrative of three men standing is Daniel's emphasis: faithful resistance against universal pressure.
8: qeretsayyata (qĕreṣayyāʾ): they accused, or literally “ate the pieces of” — an idiom for slander/denunciation derived from the image of a predator consuming. The Chaldeans (kasday) are Babylon's elite priestly-scholarly class; their denunciation of the Jews is both political (they resent the Jews' elevation, cf. Daniel 2:49) and religious (they are the guardians of Babylonian cult). The phrase kasday qeretsayyata yehudaya (Chaldeans accused Jews) sets up the ethnic and religious conflict explicitly.
9: malka lealam kheyey (malkāʾ lĕʿālam ḥĕyay): O king, live forever. This is the standard Babylonian/Persian court greeting formula. In Aramaic it uses the jussive-imperative: “may you live forever.” Its repetition at every royal appearance in Daniel (Daniel 2:4; Daniel 3:9; Daniel 5:10; Daniel 6:6, 21) functions as a literary marker of the human court's pretension to divine permanence — a pretension YHWH consistently exposes in the book.
10: The accusers restate the king's decree verbatim: this is the narrative technique of exact citation to establish the legal precision of the charge. The decree is unambiguous — no escape clause for conscience or religion. The literary function is to make the three men's refusal fully deliberate: they know exactly what they are refusing. The precision of the charge also underscores the clarity of YHWH's vindication: if the accusation is exact, the deliverance is equally exact.
11: man-hu alaha (man hûʾ ʾĕlāhāʾ): who is the god? — the rhetorical question embedded in the accusers' speech (if they do not bow, they are to be thrown into the furnace) sets the stage for the king's own version of the same question in Daniel 3:15. The Aramaic question-form with the emphatic man (who?) is used throughout Daniel for challenges to divine authority.
12: la-ilu elahach pelachain (lāʾ-ilāhāk pāleḥîn): they are not serving your gods. The verb pelach is the Aramaic term for religious service/cult-service — used for both pagan worship and YHWH-service. The accusation is precisely the violation of cultic loyalty: the three men serve a different lord. The names Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego (shadrak, meshak, aved-nego) are Babylonian court names given in 1:7 to replace their Hebrew names — but their Hebrew loyalty persists behind the Babylonian veneer.
13: ach birgaz u-chemah (ʾeḏayin nebukhad-netstsar birgaz ûḥĕmāʾ): then Nebuchadnezzar in rage and fury. The two synonyms for anger (rigza = rage/trembling, chema = hot anger/wrath) appear together for emphasis — the king's emotion is intense. Aramaic frequently uses paired synonyms for emphasis, particularly in court-speech. The repetition of his full name in the narrative context is also emphatic: the narrator does not abbreviate to “the king.”
14: hatsteva (haṣṣĕḇāʾ): Is it true? Or intentional? The Aramaic word can mean “deliberate/on purpose.” The king's question implies disbelief — he promoted these men (Daniel 1:7; Daniel 2:49), and their defiance is personally offensive. The question form (is it true) before pronouncing judgment shows a vestige of due process, though the outcome is pre-decided by the rage of Daniel 3:13, 19.
15: man-hu alaha di yesheyzevinnkon mi-yadi (man hûʾ ʾĕlāhāʾ dî yĕšêzĕvinnĕkôn mîyadî): who is the god who will deliver you from my hand? This is the theological climax of the confrontation: Nebuchadnezzar's question is a direct challenge to YHWH's existence and power. The phrase mi-yadi (from my hand) echoes the Exodus language where Pharaoh asks “who is YHWH that I should obey his voice?” (Exodus 5:2). The answer to both pharaoh and Nebuchadnezzar is the same: YHWH delivers his people from the hands of the most powerful rulers on earth.
16: la khashcheen anan lehotava-k pethgam al-dena (lāʾ-ḥaššĕḥîn ʾănaḥnāʾ lĕhātābāʾk pittĕgām ʿal-dĕnāh): we have no need to answer you in this matter. The three men's response is remarkable in its confidence: la khashchin = we are not compelled/anxious. The root khash suggests urgency or need — they feel no urgency to defend themselves. The answer is not defiance but trust: the issue does not require their advocacy because YHWH is the one who will speak through the outcome.
17: ithai elahana di anan palekheen yakhil leshezavah min-atun nura yaqidta (ʾîṯay ʾĕlāhana dî ʾănaḥnāʾ pālĕḥîn yākil lĕšêzābāh min-ʾattûn nûrāʾ yāqiḏtāʾ): if our God whom we serve is able to deliver us from the burning fiery furnace. The Aramaic conditional hn ... ithai (if there is) does not express doubt but marks the condition rhetorically; the real statement is the conviction of Daniel 3:18. yakhil (yākil): is able — the same root as the NT Greek dunamai. The ability of YHWH is affirmed even while acknowledging that deliverance is not guaranteed on human terms.
18: v'hen la yedaʿ leh malka di le-ilahacha la felechin vli-tselem dahava la sagdeen (wĕhin lāʾ yĕḏaʿ leh malkāʾ dî lēʾ-ʾĕlāhāk lāʾ pāleḥîn): and if not — be it known to you, O king, that we will not serve your gods or worship the golden image. The unconditional confession: v'hen la (and if not) — even if YHWH does not deliver us, we will not bow. This is the pivot of the entire narrative: the faith statement that does not make deliverance contingent on rescue. The theological depth is enormous: the three men trust YHWH not because he will certainly rescue them but because he is YHWH. This is the same unconditional faith as Job 13:15 (though he slay me, yet will I trust in him).
19: hash-tannei tsvey anpoh (ḥaš-tannê ṣĕwê ʾanpôhi): the form of his face was changed — literally changed the appearance of his face. The Aramaic shannei means to change/alter; the noun tsevah (form/color) is related to the word for color or appearance. The furnace temperature is increased sevenfold — a hyperbolic number expressing extreme intensification. The literary structure mirrors the defiance: the three men refused to bend; the king bends the furnace temperature. Human extremity only sets up divine demonstration.
20: gibbare chayil (gibbārê ḥayil): mighty men of valor. The superlative construction uses the noun gibbar (warrior, mighty one) with chayil (strength, wealth, army) — the most elite soldiers available. That these are the very men who are killed by the flame while throwing the three into the furnace (Daniel 3:22) is an ironic reversal: human power is destroyed by its own instrument of execution; those it kills are unharmed.
21: Bound in their clothing: sarbalehon patishehon vekarbaltehon — the list of garments includes several Persian loanwords (sarbel = tunic, patish = possibly a garment or mantle, karbela = perhaps a cap or head covering). The detail that they were thrown in fully clothed — no stripping, no preparation — makes the subsequent observation (“not even their clothing smelled of fire,” Daniel 3:27) more remarkable. The binding (kefitu, bound/tied) emphasizes helplessness: they are thrown helpless and fully clothed into the furnace.
22: gevurta malka yaqidat (giḇûrtāʾ malkāʾ yaqiḏat): the king's command was urgent/fierce. The root geburat (might, fierceness) is related to gibbar (warrior) — the same word used for the mighty men of Daniel 3:20. The irony is complete: the king's mighty command kills his own mighty men while the unarmed prisoners walk free. The causal structure — because the king's command was fierce — is the narrator's way of saying that human extremity of power is self-destructive.
23: The three men fell bound into the midst of the burning furnace. The Aramaic naflu (they fell) is the same verb root as the “falling down to worship” demanded of all peoples (Daniel 3:5-7). The three men who refused to fall in worship of the image are now literally fallen into the furnace — the irony of position. They fall not in worship but in faith, into the fire that will not harm them.
24: etdahar malka (ʾeṯdahar malkāʾ): the king was alarmed, startled. The root dahar means to terrify, to disturb greatly. The king arose in haste (behitbehala) — the same root as the “haste” of frightened men throughout Daniel (Daniel 4:5; Daniel 5:9). His question — “did we not throw three men bound into the fire?” — is forensically careful before he announces the vision of four men walking in the fire.
25: bar-elaheen (bar-ʾĕlāhîn): son of the gods. The plural elaheen (gods) reflects Nebuchadnezzar's polytheistic frame: he sees the fourth figure as a divine being, one of the elaheen. The singular bar (son of) individualizes the being: not a generic divine, but one specific divine figure. Christian readers have traditionally seen a Christophany here — the pre-incarnate Son appearing in the fire with his servants. The ambiguity between ben-elahim (son of God) and bar-elaheen (son of the gods) is theologically rich: Nebuchadnezzar can only name what he sees in his frame; the narrative's deeper meaning exceeds his frame.
26: avdohi di-el-ilai (ʿabdôhî dî-ʾēl-ʿelyāʾ): servants of the Most High God. Nebuchadnezzar calls them out using the divine title el-ilai (God Most High) — the superlative form of ilai (high/exalted). The title appears frequently in Daniel for YHWH (Daniel 2:18, 19, 20, 37, 44; Daniel 3:26, 32; Daniel 4:2, 17, 24, 25, 32, 34; Daniel 5:18, 21; Daniel 7:18, 22, 25, 27; Daniel 11:36). The Gentile king's use of el-ilai is itself a form of acknowledgment: whatever the fourth figure was, YHWH is the Most High whose servants he was protecting.
27: The verification of the miracle by the assembled officials: la shelet nura begeshmeehon (lāʾ šĕleṭ nûrāʾ begiš-mêhôn): fire had no power over their bodies. Shelet (had power/authority) is a key word in Daniel — the same root used for those who have dominion, authority, power. Fire's authority is denied: it could not exercise its jurisdiction over YHWH's servants. The four specific statements (“not singed, garments unchanged, no smell of fire”) are legally precise testimony — a formal verification of the miracle.
28: malʾakheh (malʾakhêhî): his angel. Nebuchadnezzar now identifies the fourth figure as YHWH's angel rather than a generic divine being — a shift from bar-elaheen (son of the gods) to malʾakheh (his angel/messenger). The verb sheziv (delivered) is the same root used by the three men in their declaration (Daniel 3:17: yakhil leshezavah — able to deliver). YHWH did exactly what they said he could do. Shenet pitgam malka (changed the king's word) — the king's decree was not annulled but made irrelevant: the fire obeyed YHWH, not the king.
29: Nebuchadnezzar's decree protecting the servants of YHWH: umeshta (ûmešnāʾ: to be dismembered) and baitehhon nevali yishtavon (their houses to be made a dunghill). The punishment for blasphemy against YHWH is given in terms of the standard Babylonian execution-and-property-confiscation formula. The Aramaic negida (decree/ruling) is a royal edict with universal force. Notably, Nebuchadnezzar does not convert: he issues a decree of toleration, not a confession of monotheism.
30: tzalach (ṣĕlaḥ): promoted/prospered. The verb root tsalach means to advance, prosper, or progress — used in Ezra 5-6 for the progress of the temple-building. The three men are promoted (harbit = advanced to greater status) in the province of Babylon. The narrative ends with worldly vindication — the same pattern as Daniel himself in Daniel 2: faithfulness leads to promotion. The literary structure is chiastic: forced to appear before the king (Daniel 3:13) → thrown into furnace → vindicated and promoted before the king (Daniel 3:30).
